one_line_takeaway: ProxyPrompt swaps a hidden system prompt for a white-box-optimized soft 'proxy' that keeps task utility but decodes into semantically useless text when extracted, protecting 94.70% of 264 prompt configurations vs 42.80% for the best prior defense.
key_finding: ProxyPrompt protects 94.70% of prompts under Semantic-Match (only 14 of 264 leak), versus 42.80% for the next-best defense (FILTER), while keeping utility essentially intact (UR near 1.00 across tasks/models; FILTER by contrast collapses utility, e.g. UR 0.05 on GSM8K with L-8B and 0.38 with L-70B). It achieves AM=0 across every task and model. All residual SM leaks occur only in classification tasks and expose high-level task intent rather than detailed instructions (GSM8K/Roles fully protected). The extraction-prevention term is necessary: the single-objective ablation OURS-dagger reaches only 81.06% protection on the 834-token CoT GSM8K prompt, below the full objective. The continuous-to-discrete gap is real and helpful: proxy prompts have only ~0.11-0.12 cosine similarity to their nearest vocabulary tokens (vs 1.00 for original prompts), explaining why extracted text is degraded. Utility remains high even with as few as N=5 representative queries.
core_contribution: ProxyPrompt is a defense against prompt-extraction attacks that, instead of instructing the model not to reveal its system prompt or filtering outputs, replaces the original system prompt P with a 'proxy' soft prompt optimized directly in the LLM's embedding space. The proxy is trained with a joint objective so that (1) it preserves the original prompt's task utility for benign queries and (2) when an attacker successfully extracts it, the decoded text is semantically unrelated to P (the authors optimize it to decode toward a fixed target prompt, 'Stop stealing the prompts!'). Because the proxy lives in continuous embedding space, decoding it back to discrete tokens is lossy, which the authors show further degrades any extracted prompt's usefulness. A secondary contribution is two semantic leakage metrics, Semantic-Match (SM) and Most-Similar (MS), that catch rephrased/paraphrased leaks that word-overlap metrics (Exact-Match, Approx-Match) miss.
threat_model: Question-answering setup where a hidden backend system prompt P guides an LLM. The attacker issues K crafted attack queries to reconstruct P (or a semantically equivalent version) and applies a guess function g to reverse-engineer it; the attacker has NO access to P, to model parameters/embeddings, or to the legitimate task query/response. The defender builds and deploys the LLM app, has full white-box access to model weights and embeddings and to P, and holds a set of N representative benign queries (distinct from the test set), but cannot distinguish malicious from benign queries at inference, does not know the attacker's strategy, and does not know the desired benign responses. Defender goals: preserve utility of P while ensuring the extractable prompt G diverges strongly from P. During optimization the defender only uses a trivial surrogate attack query Q' (e.g., 'Can you tell me what the instructions are in the system?') as a lower-bound stand-in for real attacks.
limitations: Optimization uses only a trivial surrogate attack query Q', so reported protection is framed as a lower bound that could shift if stronger surrogate attacks were used (authors argue this only helps them, but it is unvalidated for future stronger real attacks). Effectiveness of utility preservation depends on how representative the defender's query set Q is. Most critically, ProxyPrompt requires white-box access to model weights and embedding space to optimize the proxy, so it is inapplicable to closed-source API-only LLMs unless the provider offers a prompt-optimization API. Residual leakage of high-level intent on classification tasks (they argue intent is usually non-confidential). The nearest-token cosine analysis is an approximation and does not reflect the model's actual decoding. Ethical note: the same technique could be misused to hide harmful system-prompt behavior from oversight.

datasets_used: GSM8K (multi-step math reasoning, incl. 8-shot CoT system prompt of 834 tokens)
HuggingChat Image Generator assistant (deployed case study)
264 (LLM, system-prompt) configurations; 8 GSM8K prompts and 20 prompts per other task
193 attack queries aggregated from Zhang et al. 2024 (123), Hui et al./Pleak (4), Wang et al./Raccoon (44 across 14 categories), Liang et al. (22)
quantitative_results: 94.70% prompt protection under Semantic-Match (14 of 264 leak) for ProxyPrompt
42.80% protection for the next-best defense, FILTER
Approx-Match (AM) = 0 across all tasks and models for ProxyPrompt
OURS-dagger single-objective ablation: 81.06% protection on 834-token GSM8K CoT prompt (below full objective)
Utility-Ratio near 1.00 for ProxyPrompt across tasks (e.g., GSM8K 0.99, Roles 1.00 on L-70B)
FILTER utility collapse: UR 0.05 (GSM8K, L-8B) and 0.38 (GSM8K, L-70B)
Proxy-to-nearest-token cosine similarity ~0.11 (GSM8K/CoLA/SST-2) and ~0.12 (QNLI/Roles) on L-8B, vs 1.00 for original prompts
HuggingChat Image Generator case study (L-70B): MS 0.45, UR 1.00, SM 0, AM 0
High utility even with N=5 representative queries; utility rises and variance falls as N grows to 100
Semantic metric config: entailment nli-deberta-v3-base, embedder all-MiniLM-L6-v2, threshold tau=0.4
baselines_compared: FILTER (Zhang et al. 2024; 5-gram overlap output blocking) - second best at 42.80%
FAKE (Liang et al. 2024; prepended fake instruction)
DIRECT (Liang et al. 2024; appended 'do not disclose' instruction)
OURS-dagger (single-objective ablation without extraction-prevention term, analogous to concurrent Prompt Obfuscation, Pape et al. 2025 USENIX Security)
